expectations.evidence: File exists at outputs/AUDIT-REPORT.md (17,219 bytes). Contains a complete audit report with header, summary table, 14 findings across 4 sections, priority actions, and next steps.

expectations.text: Report covers all 9 audit areas despite the indirect phrasing
expectations.evidence: The summary table at lines 9-18 lists all 9 areas: CLAUDE.md, Rules, Skills, Sub-agents, Hooks, Permissions, Settings, MCP, Features. Each area has at least one detailed finding in the body of the report (14 findings total across 4 grouped sections).

expectations.text: Every finding includes a Source line with a docs.anthropic.com URL
expectations.evidence: All 14 findings include a **Source** line containing a docs.anthropic.com URL. Examples: finding 1 cites 'https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/memory#claudemd-files', finding 8 cites 'https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/hooks-guide', finding 13 cites 'https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/mcp#project-scope'. No finding lacks a source citation.

expectations.text: Source citations include provenance markers: (via MCP) or (via Playwright CLI)
expectations.evidence: Every Source line ends with '(via MCP)'. The report header also states 'Mode: MCP (anthropic-docs)'. All 14 findings consistently use the (via MCP) provenance marker. No (via Playwright CLI) markers appear, which is expected since the baseline run used MCP, not the Playwright CLI fallback.
All 14 findings are tagged: [fix] x5 (findings 1, 2, 8, 9, 12), [good] x4 (findings 3, 4, 5, 10), [improve] x5 (findings 6, 7, 11, 13, 14). No finding uses a tag outside the expected set.

expectations.text: Report is written in plain language — technical terms are explained briefly
expectations.evidence: The summary table explains each area in plain language (e.g. CLAUDE.md = 'Your project's instructions to Claude', Hooks = 'Automated actions before/after Claude acts', Sub-agents = 'Isolated Claude sessions for specific tasks'). Finding text uses accessible phrasing: 'Claude starts every session in this repo with zero project-specific context', 'the checkpoint skill writes logs that nobody reads unless they remember to look'. A few technical terms (frontmatter, symlink, context window) appear without explicit definitions, but their meaning is generally clear from surrounding context.

expectations.text: Report specifically identifies Claude Code features not in use and suggests which would help
expectations.evidence: The report identifies 6 unused features: (1) no project CLAUDE.md — recommends creating one with /init, (2) no .claude/rules/ directory — recommends two specific rule files, (3) no project-level sub-agents — suggests skill-tester and skill-reviewer agents, (4) no hooks configured — provides exact JSON for SessionStart hook, (5) no project settings.json — recommends creating with hooks and permissions, (6) no .mcp.json — provides exact JSON for anthropic-docs server. Each includes why it would help this specific project.

Lines 197-206 contain a '## Priority Actions' section with a numbered table of 5 actions, each with Type (fix/improve), 'What to do', and 'Why it matters' columns. Actions are ordered by priority: (1) Create CLAUDE.md, (2) Create settings.json with hook, (3) Fix stale paths, (4) Create rules, (5) Add .mcp.json.

claims.claim: The project has no CLAUDE.md, no .claude/CLAUDE.md, and no CLAUDE.local.md
claims.evidence: No CLAUDE.md files found in the skills-gc project root or .claude/ directory based on available project context.
No rules directory mentioned in git status or project structure. Consistent with the finding.

claims.claim: All six skills use proper YAML frontmatter with name, description, and appropriate optional fields
claims.evidence: Cannot verify directly from the available outputs. The report names 6 skills (checkpoint, verify, plan-challenger, perspective, cc-audit, website-audit) but the audit output alone does not include the frontmatter content of each skill.

claims.claim: settings.local.json references minimal-framework instead of skills-gc
claims.evidence: Cannot verify from grading outputs alone. The claim is specific and testable but the settings.local.json file is not included in the outputs directory.
The system-reminder lists a large number of skills from plugins, which is consistent with many plugins being active, but the exact count of 15 cannot be verified from available outputs.

claims.claim: The checkpoint skill describes a SessionStart hook that should auto-load the latest checkpoint log, but this hook does not exist
claims.evidence: Cannot verify the checkpoint skill's content or the absence of the hook from grading outputs alone.

suggestions.assertion: Report covers all 9 audit areas despite the indirect phrasing
suggestions.reason: This assertion checks coverage but not depth. A report that mentions each area in a single sentence would pass. Consider adding an assertion like 'Each audit area has at least one concrete, project-specific recommendation' to ensure the coverage is substantive.

suggestions.assertion: Every finding includes a Source line with a docs.anthropic.com URL
suggestions.reason: This checks URL presence but not URL validity. A hallucinated URL (e.g., docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/nonexistent-page) would still pass. Consider spot-checking that at least 3 cited URLs resolve to real pages.

suggestions.assertion: Report is written in plain language — technical terms are explained briefly
suggestions.reason: This is inherently subjective and hard to fail. A report could use jargon freely and still be judged 'mostly plain language'. Consider a more discriminating version: 'Every term from this list [frontmatter, hooks, MCP, sub-agents, symlink, context window] is either avoided or explained within the same sentence.'

No assertion checks factual accuracy of the findings. The report makes specific claims about the project state (e.g., 'settings.local.json references minimal-framework', '6 skills exist', 'no .claude/rules/ directory'). A hallucinated audit that sounds plausible would pass all 8 assertions. Adding one assertion like 'At least 3 factual claims in the report can be verified against actual project files' would catch fabricated audits.

eval_feedback.overall: All 8 assertions passed. The assertions cover structure, formatting, and content presence well. The main gap is verifiability — no assertion checks whether the report's factual claims about the project are actually true. For a baseline (without-skill) run, the quality is high: 14 substantive findings, all properly sourced, with actionable recommendations. The 179.6s execution time is reasonable for the scope.
